message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1, fixes applied vs main). Venv: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
The file /tmp/pass1_confirm.json is a JSON array of 22 pass-1 findings (each: issue, title, file, sev, problem, suggested_fix).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for finding INDEX 19 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_confirm.json.
Read that item (its issue number, problem, suggested_fix, file). Then VERIFY THE APPLIED FIX on the current branch: read the changed source AND its regression test (git diff vs main, or just read the file + tests), and where useful run a PoC against the venv to confirm the original problem is actually resolved.
Return: appropriate = the fix correctly addresses the root cause in a sound way (not a hack/incomplete patch/over-claim); complete = the fix fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would catch a regression. Set either false if not, and say exactly what is missing. Echo the issue number in 'issue'.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll analyze finding INDEX 19 in the confirmation file.

content.content:
{
  "issue": "120",
  "title": "Package version is hardcoded in two independent places (pyproject `version` and `__init__.__version__`), and the second feeds the reproducibility/provenance manifest",
  "file": "pyproject.toml",
  "sev": "LOW",
  "problem": "`pyproject.toml` sets `version = \"0.0.1\"` (line 7) while `src/assay_engine/__init__.py` separately hardcodes `__version__ = \"0.0.1\"` (line 28). These are two independent strings with no single source of truth \u2014 hatchling is not configured for dynamic versioning (`grep dynamic`/`hatch.version` returns nothing). This is not merely a style nit: `src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py` imports `__version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION` (line 24) and stamps it into the determinism/provenance record as `component_versions[\"engine\"]` (line 111), which is part of the hash-chained reproducibility trail. A rele",
  "suggested_fix": "Single-source the version. Either: (a) set `dynamic = [\"version\"]` in pyproject and add `[tool.hatch.version] path = \"src/assay_engine/__init__.py\"` so hatchling reads `__version__` from the module; or (b) drop the literal from `__init__.py` and derive it at runtime via `importlib.metadata.version(\"assay-engine\")`. Option (a) keeps the determinism manifest source authoritative and removes the drif"
}

content.text: Now let me verify the applied fix. Let me look at the relevant files and the diff vs main.

content.text:
The dynamic versioning works: hatchling reads `__version__` from the module, and `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine")` returns `0.0.1` matching the module. There is now a single source of truth (the `__init__.py` literal), and the determinism manifest's `_ENGINE_VERSION` reads from that same source.

However, none of the test grep results relate to version single-sourcing. Let me confirm there's truly no regression test by checking the diff of test files for any version-consistency assertion, and verify a clean build picks up the dynamic version (to be sure the editable install didn't just retain a stale value).

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git diff main -- tests/ | grep -iE "version|0\.0\.1|metadata|pyproject|dynamic|hatch"
input.description: Check test diff for version-related assertions

print('static version present:', 'version' in d['project'])

"

content.text:
Confirmed. The fix is correctly configured:
- `dynamic = ["version"]` is set, `version` static field is removed, and `[tool.hatch.version] path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"` points hatchling at the module literal.
- The venv resolves `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine")` == `assay_engine.__version__` == `0.0.1`, so single-sourcing is real.
- The determinism manifest's `_ENGINE_VERSION` reads from the now-authoritative `__init__.__version__`.

The fix correctly addresses the root cause via the recommended option (a). It is sound and not a hack.

However, there is **no regression test**. The diff of test files contains nothing about version single-sourcing — the only `version`-related test change (`test_versioner_path_for_rejects_traversal`) is about the unrelated `LocalDataVersioner` (data versioning), not package version. A regression here would be subtle: if someone later re-added a static `version = "..."` to pyproject (re-introducing the dual source / drift), nothing in the test suite would catch it. A guard test (e.g., asserting `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine") == assay_engine.__version__`, or asserting pyproject declares `dynamic` and has no static `version`) would catch the regression but does not exist.

input.reasoning: The applied fix correctly implements suggested-fix option (a) and is sound (not a hack): pyproject.toml line 7 now declares `dynamic = ["version"]` (the static `version = "0.0.1"` is removed — confirmed via tomllib that no static version key exists), and lines 76-77 add `[tool.hatch.version] path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"`, so hatchling derives the package version from the module literal `__version__` in src/assay_engine/__init__.py (line 28). This makes the module the single source of truth, which is also what baseline/determinism.py reads (`from assay_engine import __version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION`, stamped into component_versions["engine"] at line 115), so the reproducibility/provenance manifest stays authoritative. The drift is eliminated. So `appropriate=true`. But `complete=false`: there is NO regression test. The git diff of tests/ vs main contains nothing asserting version single-sourcing (the only version-named test change, test_versioner_path_for_rejects_traversal, concerns the unrelated LocalDataVersioner). If someone re-adds a static `version` to pyproject (re-introducing the dual source / drift, or causing a build conflict with dynamic), the test suite would not catch it. A guard such as `assert importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine") == assay_engine.__version__` (or asserting pyproject declares `dynamic` with no static `version`) is needed and absent.
input.evidence: pyproject.toml:7 `dynamic = ["version"]  # single-sourced from src/assay_engine/__init__.py:__version__ (#120)`; pyproject.toml:76-77 `[tool.hatch.version]` / `path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"`. src/assay_engine/__init__.py:28 `__version__ = "0.0.1"`. src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py:24 imports __version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION, line 115 `versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, ...}`. Venv PoC: `importlib.metadata.version('assay-engine')` -> 0.0.1 and `assay_engine.__version__` -> 0.0.1 (match). tomllib check: dynamic=['version'], static version present: False, hatch.version path set. `git diff main -- tests/` shows no version-single-sourcing assertion (only unrelated LocalDataVersioner traversal test).

content.text:
I've completed verification of finding INDEX 19 (issue #120).

Summary: The fix is **appropriate but incomplete**.

- **Appropriate (true):** The fix correctly implements suggested-fix option (a). `pyproject.toml` now uses `dynamic = ["version"]` with `[tool.hatch.version] path = "src/assay_engine/__init__.py"`, removing the static `version = "0.0.1"`. I confirmed via a venv PoC that `importlib.metadata.version("assay-engine")` and `assay_engine.__version__` both resolve to `0.0.1`, so the module literal is now the single source of truth — and that same literal feeds the determinism/provenance manifest (`_ENGINE_VERSION` in `baseline/determinism.py`). Root cause (dual independent version strings) is resolved soundly.

- **Incomplete (false):** No regression test exists. The `git diff` of `tests/` vs main contains no assertion tying pyproject's version to `__version__` (the only version-named test change is for the unrelated `LocalDataVersioner`). If someone re-adds a static `version` to pyproject, nothing would catch the re-introduced drift/build conflict.

Relevant files:
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/pyproject.toml (lines 7, 76-77)
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/__init__.py (line 28)
- /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py (lines 24, 115)
